Introduction to Spatial Analysis Introduction to Spatial Analysis Preface Intended Learning Outcomes

Introduction to Spatial Analysis Introduction to Spatial Analysis Preface Introduction to Spatial Analysis will introduce you to the principles and methods of spatial data analysis, with a view to developing deep understanding of how we conceptualise and model spatial patterns and relationships. You will be equipped with the knowledge and skills to identify and utilise appropriate spatial analyses, to critically assess outputs, and to communicate results to academic and non-academic audiences. You will also gain practical skills in ArcGIS Pro, and experience working with a range of environmental, social and ecological datasets. In Introduction to Spatial Analysis, we will: work with a range of spatial data types: Working with Geographic Data investigate spatial patterns: Spatial Weights, Spatial Autocorrelation, Spatial Correlation, Point Patterns and Processes model spatial relationships: Spatial Interpolation, Spatial Regression, Geographically Weighted Regression optimise locations and predict flows: Location Allocation, Spatial Interaction Modelling Chapter links will become available as the unit progresses. Intended Learning Outcomes The Intended Learning Outcomes for Introduction to Spatial Analysis are available on Canvas as a single source of truth.

Chapter 1 Working with Geographic Data 1.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Develop familiarity with ArcGIS interface. Develop skills in loading and investigating vector and raster data. Understand layer symbology and the basics of map production. Application To achieve this, we’ll import raster (temperature and precipitation layers) and vector data (country Polygons and city Points) into ArcGIS, visualise and summarise current and future climates, and produce a map. Data WorldClim temperature and precipitation data, available in [data/world-clim] [Source] Natural Earth 1:10m Cultural Vectors, available in [data/natural-earth] [Source] Esri World Cities, available in [data/esri] [Source] Tools Zonal Statistics as Table (Spatial Analyst), Cell Statistics (Spatial Analyst Tools), Select Layer By Attribute (Data Management Tools) 1.2 Practical During this practical… [1] Open ArcGIS Pro and become familiar with layout [2] Add vector data (countries + cities) [3] Filter cities to >5 million population using Select Layer By Attribute [n=54, Export] [4] Add example of raster data e.g., wc2.1_10m_tavg_01.tif - talk about generalisation and critical cartography e.g., Crampton and Krygier (2005) [5] Use Cell Statistics to average all the temperature data [data/world-clim/wc2.1_10m_tavg] [6] Repeat for precipitation [data/world-clim/wc2.1_10m_prec] [7] Use Zonal Statistics as Table (country - temperature) [8] Use Zonal Statistics as Table (city - temperature) [9] Symbolise points based on temperature, population … [10] Make a beautiful map 1.3 Extra In the practical we’ve been working with historical climate data (1970 - 2000) from WorldClim 2, described by Fick and Hijmans (2017). WorldClim 2 also includes future climate projections, derived from CMIP6 (Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6), a project which combines outputs from a range of global climate models (GCM) for different scenarios of societal development, known as Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSPs). A good overview of the SSPs is provided here. Download the monthly average temperature datasets [tx] for 2081-2100 using the U.K. Earth System Model (UKESM1-0-LL, Sellar et al. (2019)) at a 10-minute spatial resolution here. For SSP1-2.6 (Sustainable), SSP2-4.5 (Middle of the road), SSP3-7.0 (Regional rivalry) and SSP5-8.5 (Fossil-Fueled Development), calculate the average annual monthly temperature using Cell Statistics. Which world cities (>5 million population) are expected to see the greatest increase in temperature by 2081 - 2100? You may want to use Zonal Statistics again… References

Chapter 3 Spatial Autocorrelation 3.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Understand why spatial autocorrelation needs to be considered when analysing spatial data. Understand the techniques we can use to assess for global and local spatial autocorrelation, including the underlying mathematics and assumptions. Test for spatial autocorrelation in ArcGIS and interpret outputs correctly. Application To that end, we’ll use county-level data from the 2024 US General election to investigate global and local spatial autocorrelation. Chapter 4 Spatial Correlation 4.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Understand how spatial autocorrelation is also important when studying correlation between multiple variables. Understand how we might adapt traditional measures of correlation (e.g., Pearson’s \(r\)) when working with spatial data. Assess the varying performance of correlation methods when spatial dependence is included/excluded. Application To that end, we’ll use county-level data from the 2024 US General election used in the previous practical and demographic data from the same level, to assess the relationship between the variables, taking the topological relationship among observations into account. Data In addition to the county-level data used in the previous practical, we will be using a range of county-level demographic datasets. These are available in [data/us-census] as follows: Poverty indicators and education statistics [Source] [Documentation] Demographics [Source] [Documentation] For those of you interested in further analysis (see Extra), I have also compiled data on: Mortality [Source] Unemployment [Source] Internet adoption [Source] Tools Bivariate Spatial Association (Lee’s L) (Spatial Statistics) 4.2 Practical [TO DO] Determine which datasets should be used for the main analysis, and which to save for “A little more?” Main (GOP percent) - Demographics [*] ——- positive example (white %) - Education [*] ———- negative example (bachelor degree %) - Poverty [*] ———— L ~ R example (median income) A little more: - Mortality rate ——— e.g., median income vs. mortality rate - Unemployment ———– e.g., high school graduate % vs. unemployment rate - Internet adoption —— e.g., internet adoption vs. bachelor degree % Remove: - Overdose deaths - Diabetes Poverty descriptors PCTPOVALL_2023 = Poverty Percent, All Ages MEDHHINC_2023 = Median Household Income [1] Load contiguous county data from Week 3 (or produce if not saved) [2] If not joined to 2024 election data, repeat this step… [3] Load the csv files for poverty, education, demographics [4] This time, lets join based on the FIPS code (5-digit Federal Information Processing Series)… > Introduce data types here - Text (String) vs. Numeric # To achieve this, let’s convert the Text GEOID to numeric [A] Add Field -> GEOID_int -> Long (32-bit integer) -> Field Precision (5) [B] Attribute Table -> Calculate Field -> int(!GEOID!) -> Now stored as numeric (removing leading 0) [C] Join based on GEOID_int to FIPS_code [5] Symbology, visualisation … [6] Test for spatial autocorrelation first… For gop_perc, we know from Week 3 that global autocorrelation is present, and have tested this locally Test for autocorrelation for some variables of your choice, point students towards: -> White % -> Bachelor degree % -> Median income [7] Run Bivariate Spatial Association (Lee’s L) (Spatial Statistics), using GOP percent as the constant, comparing to: -> White % -> Bachelor degree % -> Median income [8] Interpretation and understanding: *** Key point: “How much of this relationship survives once we account for spatial structure?” *** Understanding: What are key differences between Pearson Correlation and Lee’s L? *** How do we interpret the global and local results? -> Consistent R > L across many bivariate comparison [below] -> County-level socioeconomic variables tend to be globally correlated but only moderately spatially cross-correlated -> Indicative of smooth, large-scale monotonic trends across ~3,000 counties -> County-level socioeconomic data has stronger global correlation structure than neighbor-based spatial coupling structure. From AI: - Lee’s L > Pearson \(r\): The spatial arrangement of your variables positively reinforces their correlation. In other words, similar values of both variables are clustered together in the same geographic neighborhoods, artificially “boosting” the apparent relationship when viewed through a spatial lens. - Lee’s L < Pearson \(r\): Spatial autocorrelation is working against the overall linear relationship, meaning your variables are less spatially clustered than their raw correlation would suggest. - If L is close to Pearson’s r, the spatial arrangement adds little beyond the usual linear association, so the two variables correlate in a fairly ordinary way. Pearson: Do high X and high Y occur in the same place? Lee’s L: Do places surrounded by high X also tend to be surrounded by high Y? If the second is stronger than the first = L > R (rare) [9] Sensitivity testing of spatial weights matrix -> How do the correlations change as a distance or k-neighbours based approach is used? 4.3 Extra I’ve provided you with some other county-level datasets, including age-standardised mortality rate, unemployment rate, and high-speed internet availability. Chapter 5 Point Patterns 5.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Understand the key differences between point-pattern analysis and point-process modelling. Understand common approaches used to analyse the spatial distribution of point data. Apply these approaches to an ecological dataset. Application To that end, we’ll analyse the patterns in a Point dataset, using research-grade species observations of Panthera tigris from iNaturalist, obtained via the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF). Data The data for this practical are available in [data/gbif] as follows: Species observations of Panthera tigris (N=4449) obtained via GBIF [Source] Protected areas of India (National Parks and Wildlife Sanctuaries), built from OpenStreetMap data [Source] Tools Point Patterns Kernel Density (Spatial Analyst Tools), Mean Center (Spatial Statistics Tools), Median Center (Spatial Statistics Tools), Standard Distance (Spatial Statistics Tools), Directional Distribution (Standard Deviational Ellipse) (Spatial Statistics Tools), Average Nearest Neighbor (Spatial Statistics), Multi-Distance Spatial Cluster Analysis (Ripley’s K Function) (Spatial Statistics), Find Point Clusters (GeoAnalytics Server Tools) Data Management Spatial Join (Analysis Tools), Clip (Analysis Tools), Generate Tessellation (Data Management Tools), Minimum Bounding Geometry (Data Management), Create Random Points (Data Management), XY Table To Point (Data Management Tools) 5.2 Practical Structure: -> Visualising Points -> Centrography -> Randomness and clustering -> Identifying clusters Discussion points: -> Ensure the practical finishes with a natural progression to point-process modelling i.e., all of this is great, and tells us about the pattern of points, but not how that pattern arises - outside the scope of this unit, but an ecological example in Spatial Ecology. -> Observation Window -> Uncertainty (n=125 at single location, +/- 30 km, importance at our scale of analysis?) -> DON’T discuss presence-absence problems here, as this will be better suited to the interpolation practical [1] View data (.csv) [2] Load to ArcGIS and convert to Point - XY Table to Points not working, even with minimal and super-minimal (lat-lon only) examples -[Solution] Pasting data to a new csv works - bodge! [3] Subset to area of interest (Indian subcontinent); -> Select by Attributes (ADMIN = ‘India’ OR ADMIN = ‘Nepal’ OR ADMIN = ‘Bhutan’ OR ADMIN = ‘Bangladesh’) -> Either Export Selection (or keep selected) -> Clip: Input = Tiger Points, Feature = Natural Earth (selected record) [Topics] Discuss Spatial Joins / Overlay / Clip here? [TO DO] Remove non-contiguous part? Multi-part to single-part [4] Dissolve our area of interest (Indian Subcontinent): -> Dissolve tool - Natural Earth - Use the selected records -> Dissolve Fields: leave blank = all features together -> Statistics Fields: also blank (just using area to define study extent), but could do statistics here e.g., POS_EST [SUM], GDP_MD [Mean] [Topics] Discuss polygon-representation issues i.e., does the Tiger recognise political boundaries? [5] Projection In the previous practicals, we’ve largely been dealing with topological issues (i.e., neighbouring, bordering) and haven’t therefore had to deal with geometric issues i.e., areas, distances (link to Understanding GIS, Week 2) In this practical, we are considering those issues i.e., are points clustered or randomly dispersed? As a result, the choice of projection is an important one! How do we select: Best option (for global analysis or if in doubt) = ellipsoidal / geodesic measurement Smaller areas, projected CRS, selecting a property to preserve (conformal, equal area, equidistant) For individual countries, we can often use the national grid e.g., the CRS is British National Grid (conformal), but is sufficiently accurate for area and distance calculations For area, we can select via: The Projection Wizard -> Copy WKT to text, save as .prj -> Import Coordinate System New suggested projected coordinate system -> Extent of a layer -> Property (conformal, equal-area, etc) [6A] Generate tesselation (2000 km2) [6B] Spatial join (Target = tesselate, Join = points, field name = county, one to one, completely contains [or intersects]) [Topics] Symbology effects, Graduated Colours (using quantiles etc) or Unclassed Colours (min to max) [!] Important, because when looking at the points in isolation (at the sub-continent scale), there are lots of high observation clusters [!] When tesselated, there is one-two areas that stands out (>500 observations) [7] Kernel Density -> Population field = NONE ? -> Cell size e.g., 10_000 m -> Explore changing search radius e.g., 20_000 to 200_000 m2 -> Method = Geodesic (incorporting Earth shape) -> [TO CHECK] Effects of barriers - does influence intensity… -> [NOTE] This only produces values within the data range (x-y) -> This process creates static layers, but we can also use a dynamic approach via Symbology -> More flexible, but less robust i.e., Radius is in screen units (points), rather than map units (meters, feet, etc) -> [CHECK] Constant vs. Dynamic [8] Mean Center and Median Center -> Center of Mass, discrepancy of mean-median caused by skew -> Median = 50% of points above+below point, and left+right -> For the tiger dataset, the mean is skewed north by Himalayan observations [9] Standard Distance tool -> Produces 559904.230305 m for Indian dataset i.e., on average, observations are ~560 km apart -> Link back to projection here - uses the units of projection, so if we use a geographic projection, units in degrees [10] Directional Distribution (Standard Deviational Ellipse) -> This provides information on the center, dispersion, and orientation -> CenterX and CentreY is identical to outputs of mean centre and standard distance tools -> Shape indicates wider disperson along N-S axis (YStdDist) vs. E-W axis (XStdDist), with rotation of ~12deg. -> This probably reflects outlier points in Bangladesh, Assam etc, coupled with points in SW India [11] Average Nearest Neighbour (Clark-Evans R) -> Very straightforward implementation (use projected data, with Euclidean distance) -> Interpretation simple, with html output [TO DO] Communicate analytical approach, z-score [12] Multi-Distance Spatial Cluster Analysis (Ripley’s K) -> Very slow with high number of permutations, and very slow even with no permutations -> Highly sensitive to “study area” method, see: https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/multi-distance-spatial-cluster-analysis.html?tabs=dialog [TO HIGHLIGHT] Observation Window [TO INVESTIGATE] Boundary correction [UNDERSTAND] The Ripley family - how is the ‘K’ in ArcGIS different from G and F in “Geographic Data Science with Python” This is a reference to Ripley (1988). Ripley’s G (Nearest Neighbor Event-to-Event)What it does: It calculates the distance from each point to its nearest neighboring point and plots the cumulative distribution of these distances.Use case: It is perfect for testing if specific features (like specific plant species or retail store locations) are clustered together. If \(G(d)\) shows 80% of points have a nearest neighbor within 10 meters, it indicates tight clustering.ArcGIS Tool: Average Nearest Neighbor or evaluated using Spatial Autocorrelation. Ripley’s F (Empty Space or Point-to-Event)What it does: Instead of measuring from feature to feature, it measures the distance from a set of randomly placed points (empty spaces) to the nearest feature.Use case: It measures the “gaps” between features. For example, it can help determine if a city has an even distribution of parks by measuring how far a randomly selected residential street is from the nearest green space 5.4 Resources Baddeley, A., Rubak, E., & Turner, R. Chapter 6 Interpolation 6.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Introduce geostatistical approaches for analysing spatial data. Understand how common spatial interpolation techniques work. Perform interpolation in ArcGIS and become familiar with common applications e.g., for prediction, as an input for spatial regression. Application To achieve this, we’ll apply interpolation techniques to Point samples of global plant trait data (leaf area, plant height), modelled by Lusk et al. (2026). Our study area is Peru, which features extreme gradients in climate and topography, which are likely to be reflected in spatial gradients in plant traits. Data This practical uses global plant trait data derived from crowdsourced biodiversity monitoring and Earth observation, available in [data/global-plant-traits] [Paper] [Source] Tools Extract Values to Points (Spatial Analyst Tools), IDW (Geostatistical Analyst Tools), Empirical Bayesian Kriging (Geostatistical Analyst Tools), Geostatistical Wizard, Exploratory Interpolation (Geostatistical Analyst Tools) 6.2 Practical Interpolation techniques are used to estimate unknown values at new locations based on known measurements from surrounding sample points. We could use our ecological dataset from Practical 5 here, but it’s not an ideal example, for a few reasons: [1] Our Points don’t have corresponding values that we would want to predict i.e., characteristics of each observation [2] Our Points represent individual observations, so to interpolate, we would have to aggregate these in some way i.e., # observations with a certain distance of some representative locations (schematic?) [3] Fundamentally, the distribution of Tiger observations is clearly non-continuous, found mostly in designated reserves. Interpolation will probably do a poor job at capturing this spatial pattern, and instead, we’d probably want to use a species distribution model (Link to Spatial Ecology) [4] As a final reason, our data are Presence-Only data (i.e., observations of Tigers), and we have no Absence-Data, see: - https://doi.org/10.1111/jbi.14622 - https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1472-4642.2011.00755.x Instead, we are going to apply interpolation to a dataset where we assume the spatial pattern is continuous, and where we have a variable we want to predict. [1] Load leaf area dataset into ArcGIS, and Natural-Earth dataset [2] Generate random points, within bounding geometry of Peru, excluding marine-areas (100?) -> Minimum Bounding Geometry (envelop) -> Clip to NE data -> Create Random Points [3] Extract raster value [Extract Values to Points] [4] Remove any rows where values are invalid (-9999) [A] Manually via the Attribute Table [B] Select by Attribute (RASTERVALU <> -9999, Export selection) [5] We now have a valid dataset (all points within bounding geometry, on land, with a SLA value). However, we are again doing geometry calculations today (i.e., predicting values at locations), so we need to working with data that is suitably projected, rather than using geographic data. -> UTM 18S is probably an appropriate selection here -> Use Project tool [6] First, IDW: [Options] Effects of Power + search radius —————– General approach: Resources: https://doi.org/10.1111/jen.13455, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2021.109501 —————– Thoughts: -> Geostatistical Wizard! Talk through the separate tools, but do all the calculations in the GW? -> Semi-variogram construction (and models) -> Focus on a subset of interpolation methods, but do them really well? i.e., IDW + Kriging (non- and geostatistical) + Natural Neighbour -> Global vs. local, exact vs. approximate… —————– Tools: [A] Inverse distance weighting [B] Various forms of Kriging [Empirical Bayesian Kriging] -> Universal? [incorporating a trend function] -> Ordinary? [assumption of stationarity i.e., the statistical properties of the data do not change across space. It assumes the underlying mean is constant but unknown] -> We should use universal, because we *would* expect non-stationarity i.e., trends in climate, elevation... -> but we should run both [C] Trend -> Even weirder than Spline! [D] Spline -> Funky output! [E] Natural Neighbour -> Output constrained to Points (convex hull) [NOT] co-kriging, uses one or more correlated secondary (auxiliary) variables [NOT] any regression, RF, entropy methods —————– Finish By comparing the interpolation output (e.g., Universal Kriging) with the “true” values from the input data i.e., residual. Need to do so cautiously, acknowledging that the input is also a model. However, this is important for validation of interpolation outputs - how well does our prediction match real empirical measurements? Fundamentally, interpolation is important when we are studying something that will expected to vary continuously through space (even if it goes to 0) e.g., soil depth, elevation, species richness, but only have measurements of that variable at discrete locations. —————– Alternative dataset ideas Something like this (but not this): https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-024-03098-2 AVONET - bird traits? https://doi.org/10.1111/ele.13898 TRY plant trait database https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14904 How about https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-68996-y, random sampling…, pick a location with an interesting ecological gradient (Peru, Iberia?) ArcGIS raster issues [+32768] —————– Original approach Need to go from Points to a variable at each location… [1] Collect Events tool… dataset uncertainty / generalisation… [1] Generate Near Table -> Summarize -> Join by ID [1] Find Point Clusters - DBSCAN (50 km, minimum 10 observations) [2] Case Field not working, because ‘CLUSTER_ID’ is double, not integer… [!!!] I don’t think the tiger dataset is a great example because: [a] At this scale, we really need a species distribution model [b] Tigers seem to be mostly in the nature reserves, and any interpolation method will struggle with that… 6.3 Extra 6.4 Resources Oliver, M.A. and Webster, R. Chapter 9 Location Allocation 9.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Understand the aims and theory of location allocation models. Understand the methods and criteria used for location allocation. Investigate the sensitivity of location allocations to those methods-criteria. Application To that end, we’ll use location allocation models to optimise locations for new defibrillators, using population demand and the transport network as inputs. Our analysis is focused on the Western Isles of Scotland, given links between socioeconomic deprivation, urban-ruralness and defibrillator access (Burgoine et al., 2024). For every minute that someone’s in cardiac arrest without receiving CPR and having a defibrillator used on them, their chance of survival decreases by 10%. Optimising defibrillator locations to minimise response times or maximise coverage could save lives. Data Defibrillator locations listed on the National Defibrillator Network, managed by the British Heart Foundation and associates (The Circuit), available in [data/bhf] [Source] Ordnance Survey Open Roads transport network, available in [data/os-open-roads] [Source] UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology (UKCEH) gridded population at 1 km resolution (2021), available in [data/ukceh] [Source] ONS boundaries data, filtered to the Western Isles, available in [data/ons/national] [Source] Tools Build Network (Network Analyst Tools), Make Location-Allocation Analysis Layer (Network Analyst Tools), Near (Analysis Tools), Snap (Editing Tools), Round Up (Spatial Analyst Tools), Int (Spatial Analyst Tools), Create Feature Dataset (Data Management Tools) 9.2 Practical ————– Resources Church: https://www.geos.ed.ac.uk/~gisteac/gis_book_abridged/files/ch20.pdf R package: https://github.com/giacfalk/locationallocation Coastline: https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/ons::countries-december-2023-boundaries-uk-bfe/explore?location=54.650000%2C-3.250000%2C5 ————— Initial Processing [1] Load road network, population grid and islands shapefile - [all BNG, subset to Western Isles] [2] Load defibrillator csv and convert to features (WGS_1984), and then project to BNG [3] Clean defibrillator dataset (keep valid, align to network) -> Near Tool (distance defib -> road network, modifies input) -> Visualise Statistics -> Select by Attributes (NEAR_DIST > 100, n=15) - DELETE (coordinate errors?) -> Snap (Editing Tools, modifies input, Type = Edge, Distance = 100) Snap nearby to the network (< 100 m) -> Delete others -> Subset to defibrillators with 24 hour access? [4] Clean Demand points … Option 1 -> Raster to Point (population) -> Near Tool (investigate via Symbology) -> A reasonable cutoff would be 700 m via Pythagoras: sqrt(0.5^2 + 0.5^2) = 0.7071 km -> Snap (Distance = 700 i.e., generalized to 1 km, so we “know” that the population is within a ~700 m radius) OR Option 2 -> Round Up (Spatial Analyst) -> int(), to convert from float to int via truncation (no effect, as already rounded up) -> Raster to Points (Centroid) -> Buffer (500 m) -> Minimum Bounding Geometry (Envelop) = grid_code contains the number of people per 1 km cell [!] This is slightly complicated but is better than Raster to Polygon, which merges neighbouring cells with the same value -> Create Random Points (# within cell) -> Near Tool -> Snap to within 1000 m -> Delete the rest [Thoughts] At best both options are an approximation of where people live, but Option 1 does produce the correct # people within each 1 km grid square, and perhaps more realistic than using a centroid location (snapped to the network). Option 1 is a much simpler approach, and less representative of the actual locations (probably), but would minimise computation time. Given the aim of the practical (understanding techniques), Option 1 is sufficient. [5] For efficiency, we are going to focus on a single island, so use Clip to subset all datasets to Isle of Mull (Name = Mull) -> Roads (network), population points (demand, n=?), defibrillator locations (required / candidate locations, n=38) [6] Create Feature Dataset (empty) -> To run Location Allocation in ArcGIS, we can’t use a simple features layer, we need to use a Feature Dataset, which has stricter rules regarding projection [7] Import features: -> Right click in Catalog Pane -> Import -> Road network (Feature Class to Geodatabase) [8] Create Network Dataset (which defines the rules and sources of your network) = road_model [9] Build Network (processing action that enforces those rules, maps connectivity, and generates the data structure needed for routing) [*] Initial exploratory analysis - Closest Facility -> Make Closest Facility Analysis Layer -> Import Incidents (population) -> Import Facilities (defib) -> Number of facilities to find = 1 -> Run, Output: - Mean distance = 2739.060346368907 - Median distance = 2239.7073992125115 - Q3 = 3887.6913633425847 - Max = 10038.716195167184 [!] This is quite laborious and not necessary, because the baseline P-median run provides us with these distances ====================================== ### P-median approach i.e., minimize the total distance or time traveled by users to their nearest facility, weighted by the amount of demand or “How do we minimise average distance for everyone?” ====================================== [10] Make Location-Allocation Analysis layer (~5 mins) -> Network Data Source = road_model -> Name = la_pmedian_mull [11] Location-Allocation Layer tab -> Input Data group -> Import Facilities (defib) -> Property FacilityType = Required i.e., current sites always included in the solution [12] Location-Allocation Layer tab -> Input Data group -> Import Demand Points (population) -> Property Weight = grid_code (the population value from the raster) [13] First run using current configuration -> Set Facilities (#) = number of features in defib (for Mull, n = 38) -> Output: Sum of allocated weight in “Demand Points”: 3050.685136. Sum of valid unallocated weight in “Demand Points”: 0.000000. Sum of allocated weighted impedance in “Demand Points”: 363134.112470. Sum of valid unallocated weight in “Demand Points”: 1093.134344. Coverage = 64% i.e., reducing the cutoff reduces coverage, but this is still comparable to the baseline, even with a more restrictive cutoff (500 m vs. 1 km) ————— To finish reiterate differences between p-median and maximise coverage. What are they modelling and how does this influence interpretation action? 9.3 Extra [*] Using the maximum coverage approach, how many facilities do we need to ensure 95% of the population are within 1 km of an AED? baseline = 62% N=38 = 83% N=50: Sum of allocated weight in “Demand Points”: 2672.349411 = 87% … N=100: Sum of allocated weight in “Demand Points”: 2937.332799 = 96% [*] How do our results compare to other islands? Run for Jura… [Q] What key population metric are we not including? - non-resident population i.e., tourists! [Q] What are some weaknesses of our approach? - population generalisation - using distance as a metric, rather than travel time - not a full constraints analysis i.e., which locations are suitable for AEDs? ==================================================== ————— Using Maximise Coverage and Minimise Facilities Sum of allocated weight in “Demand Points”: 2985.974942. Sum of allocated weighted impedance in “Demand Points”: 1109527.936652. Sum of valid unallocated weight in “Demand Points”: 64.710195. Number of chosen facilities in solution: 156. Coverage = 97.87% For Maximise Coverage, there are a fixed number of facilities and demand outside the cutoff is considered uncovered, regardless of how close it is beyond the threshold. Maximise Coverage and Minimise Facilities = “What is the smallest number of facilities needed to achieve this level of coverage?”” ————— Notes When running with the full Western Isles dataset, p-median error: [!] One or more locations in “Demand Points” could not be reached by one or more locations in “Facilities”. The network may have areas that are disconnected, unbuilt, or disconnected due to turn restrictions, restriction attributes, or barriers. Incomplete network connectivity may result in a non optimal solution. —– Data error rather than model error (road network broken at some bridges) 9.4 Resources Hakimi, S.L. (1965). Chapter 10 Spatial Interaction Modelling 10.1 Introduction Aims The aims of this practical are to: Understand the aims and common applications of spatial interaction (gravity) models. Understand the design and calibration of spatial interaction models. Apply spatial interaction models to a contemporary sociological issue. Application To that end, we’ll develop spatial interaction models to predict internal migration flows between local authorities in England and Wales. Data All data for this practical have been sourced from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) at the local authority level and are found in [data/ons/local-authority] as follows: ONS internal migration flows for local authorities in England and Wales (2021) [Source] ONS district geometries (2021) [Source] ONS gross domestic product (GDP) per head at current market prices [Source] ONS population [Source] Tools Feature To Point (Data Management Tools), Generate Near Table (Analysis Tools), Generalized Linear Regression (GeoAnalytics Desktop Tools), Notebooks in ArcGIS Pro 10.2 Practical TO DO: Find some additional datasets for the “Something more?” section Update the Python notebook code to use a relative file path ————— Concepts: -> Newton’s Law of Universal Gravitation -> Spatial flows between an origin and a destination are modelled as proportional to their gravitational force and inversely related to their spatial separation ————— Sources: -> [!] https://rpubs.com/adam_dennett/376877 -> [!] https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10052338/7/Dennett_38-41-115-1-10-20181111.pdf -> https://openjournals.wu.ac.at/region/paper_175/175.html -> https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/simodels/vignettes/simodels.html -> https://robinlovelace.net/simodels/articles/sims-first-principles.html -> https://repository.ncrm.ac.uk/resources/online/all/spatial_interaction_modelling -> https://researchbriefings.files.parliament.uk/documents/SN06077/SN06077.pdf -> https://worldmigrationreport.iom.int/msite/wmr-2024-interactive/ -> https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0286244 -> https://rpubs.com/adam_dennett/259068 ————— Data: -> Internal migration flows: https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/internalmigrationinenglandandwales -> District geometries (2021): https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/ons::local-authority-districts-december-2021-boundaries-gb-bfc/explore?location=54.734360%2C-2.584695%2C7 -> GDP (Gross domestic product per head at current market prices): https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/gdp-by-local-authority/editions/time-series/versions/2 -> Population: https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/estimatesofthepopulationforenglandandwales ————— Summary Produce bilateral distances and link origin-destination attributes for England and Wales local authority data Produce unconstrained (or total constrained) model Calibrate the model using Poisson regression Introduce production-, attraction- and doubly-constrained models Run constrained models using Python Notebook and statsmodels glm() Something more? Find the variables which best predict flow.. [1] Load Local Authority districts [2] Return Centroids (Feature to Point): -> Discuss: sensitivity to feature geometry -> Calculate Geometry Attributes [3] Generate Near Table (links based on ID), using geodesic measurement, [!] save in Geodatabase (editable) [4] Load Gross domestic product (gdp) and population (pop) csv’s and use Join Field to add to Centroids data [5] Join Field, keeping the origin information (IN_FID), using ‘Use Field Mapping’ to select attributes of interest and updating names: - orig_LAD21CD (unique ID) - orig_LAD21NM (name) - orig_gdp - orig_pop - orig_long (or E) - orig_lat (or N) [6] Repeat for destinations (NEAR_ID): - dest_LAD21CD (unique ID) - dest_LAD21NM (name) - dest_gdp - dest_pop - dest_long (or E) - dest_lat (or N) [7] Create origin-destination field in the near table - !orig_LAD21CD! + “-” + !dest_LAD21C! [8] Load internal migration data (flow) and inspect - Histogram - Min-Max [9] Join Field, based on origin-destination field, transferring flow (time required) [10] Data cleaning: -> Select by Attributes and remove rows: - orig_pop, dest_pop = 0 - orig_gdp, dest_gdp = 0 - flow is null -> Result is (<) ~80_000 rows [11] Visualise some flow data - Select by Attributes i.e., flow > 1_000 (n=~450) - XY to Line (ORIG_LON - Size by flow [REMOVE] as a complex dataset ————— Data summary We now have all the data we need to begin modelling… similar format to Dennett (2018): https://rpubs.com/adam_dennett/376877 Present data columns: -> Origin (orig_LAD21CD) -> Destination (dest_LAD21CD) -> Flow (flow, # people) -> Origin (vi) population (orig_pop) -> Destination (wj) population (dest_pop) -> Origin GDP (orig_gdp) -> Destination GDP (dest_gdp) -> Distance (NEAR_DIST, meters) [12] Exploratory plots - any correlations? ————— Model description Classic multiplicative gravity model: (EQ1) \(T_{ij} = k \frac{V_i^μ W_j^α}{d_{ij}^{\beta}}\) Can also be written from Wilson (1971) as: (EQ2) \(T_{ij} = kV_i^μ W_j^αd_{ij}^{-\beta}\) The latter is mathematically identical, but removes the fraction (simpler). From Dennett: “The flows between an origin and destination are proportional to the product of the mass of the origin and destination and inversely proportional to the distance between them. As origin and destination masses increase, flows increase, but as distance increases, flows decrease, and vice versa.” Here: \(T_{ij}\) is the predicted flow between origins and destinations. \(V\) is the origin “emissiveness” i.e., origin population and salaries (e.g., ORIG_POP, ORIG_GDP, ORIG_GDP_CAP) \(W\) is the destination “attractiveness” i.e., destination population and salaries (e.g., DEST_POP, DEST_GDP, DEST_GDP_CAP) \(d\) is the cost of flows i.e., distance (NEAR_DIST) \(k\), \(μ\), \(α\), \(\beta\) are model parameters to be estimated These parameters relate to the scaling effect or importance of the variables they are associated with. We can start with some basic parameters i.e., effects scale in a linear fashion. For a 1 unit increase in population, we might expect a 1 unit increase in flows of people from that origin. Newtons gravity uses a power law of \(\beta=-2\), so here are the starting values: \(k=1\), \(μ=1\), \(α=1\), \(\beta=-2\) For \(V\) (origin “emissiveness”) we will use origin population (orig_pop) For \(W\) (destination “attractiveness”), we will use destination GDP per capita (dest_gdp) For \(d\), we use \(d^\beta\), where \(d\) = NEAR_DIST and \(\beta=-2\) gm1 = \(V\) * \(W\) * \(d\) ————— Modelling [13] Add Field (gm1), Float, following equation (distance in km) pow(!orig_pop!, 1) * pow(!dest_gdp!, 1) * pow(!NEAR_DIST!, 1000, -2) This produces an unconstrained output (unitless), as we have not used the k factor, which converts our model to a total constrained version by scaling to match the total number of flows i.e., sum of all observed flows divided by the sum of all of the other elements in the model. [14] Using Explore Statistics, return the sum of flow (102228926.73000628) and gm1 (3912.65851170132), and then calculate a new field incorporating the scale factor (gm1_scale) Data = 3698001.8812999986 (# internal migrations) Model = ~63224.55841802744 (sum of gm1) !gm1! * (3698001.8812999986 / 63224.55841802744) [!] Note: we can’t do this all via Add Field, as this is a row-wise calculation [15] Model evaluation First, check the total of gm1_scale matches the total flow (it does!), so our model is total constrained [!] However, the output is pretty abysmal here (R2=0.07, Create Chart, Scatter Plot), because we haven’t tuned our parameters! ————— Tuning parameters If we log both sides of the equation above, we get the following: (EQ3) \(\mathrm{ln}T_{ij}=k+μ\mathrm{ln}V_i+α\mathrm{ln}W_j−β\mathrm{ln}d_{ij}\) This is basically a regression model! However, there are problems with using a log-linear approach, and instead we use Poisson regression, which is better for skewed distributions (like our flow data) [16] Visualise Statistics - Histrogram - Flow So our Poisson regression looks like the following: (EQ4) \(λ_{ij}=\mathrm{exp}(k+μ\mathrm{ln}V_i+α\mathrm{ln}W_j−β\mathrm{ln}d_{ij})\) [17] First, log the independent variables using Add Field -> math.log(!orig_pop!) -> math.log(!dest_gdp!) -> math.log(!NEAR_DIST! / 1000) # converting to km -> * see section below on math.log() [18] Run Generalized Linear Regression (GeoAnalytics Desktop Tools) -> Must use GDT version, as this allows for table inputs -> Flow (dependent) -> Logged variables (independent) -> Count (Poisson) [19] Evaluate and interpret coefficients Variable ——– Coefficients —- Standard error —- t-Statistic —- Probability orig_pop_log —- 0.9985 ———- 0.0009 ———– 1071.6299 —— 0 dest_gdp_log —- 0.2862 ———- 0.0006 ———– 446.6138 ——- 0 near_dist_log — -1.2354 ——— 0.0005 ———– -2611.7296 —– 0 Intercept ——- -5.4022 ——— 0.0132 ———– -409.3151 —— 0 [20] Produce gm2 (calibrated model) and evaluate performance using Create Chart, Scatter Plot Equation: math.exp(-5.4022+(0.9985!orig_pop_log!)+(0.2862!dest_gdp_log!)-(abs(-1.2354)*!near_dist_log!)) Performance: R2 = 0.45 [21] Introduce Origin constrained models -> This is technically possible in ArcGIS but not easy, requiring us to use Encode Field to create dummy variables for orig_LAD21CD. -> Generalised Linear Regression (tool) struggles with this number of variables (~300) with multicollinearity issues -> Also very difficult to manually apply the coefficients to a regression equations -> Instead, load a Python notebook and use the appendix code to perform GLR and predict (gm3s), saving output to csv [*] Important, while I don’t expect the students to write the code independently, I should introduce it in a peicemeal fashion, explaining what each line does. [22] Load predictions csv and assess performance (flow_int vs. gm3): -> R2 = 0.57 -> Sum of modelled flows for each origin matches measured flows [23] Discuss destination-constrained and doubly-constrained models [*] Ask students to run a destination-constrained model, adapting the Python code to produce gm4? 10.3 Extra Options: -> Add additional predictors e.g., median earnings rather than GDP, unemployment rate, housing affordability, share of working-age population - We can do this for the unconstrained, production or attraction constrained models (i.e., more predictors for the origin or destination). but not for the doubly-constrained model, as this takes into account the distance only (?). -> Include contiguity: - Create numeric ID for LA geometries using SequentialNumber() - Create Spatial Weights Matrix (Contiguity Edges Corners) - Convert Spatial Weights Matrix to Table - Join SWM table to LA geometries: = NUMERIC_ID (origin) to numeric_ID: orig_LAD21CD = NID (destination) to numeric_ID: dest_LAD21CD - Create origin-destination field - Create binary bordering field (contig = 1) - Join Field: NEAR_TABLE to SVWM based on origin-destination, linking contig = This produces 1 (bordering) or (not bordering) - Calculate Field, update contig using: 0 if !contig! is None else !contig! - Add contiguity to the model ————— Log issues Python: math.log() uses e as the default R: log() uses exp(1) as the default, which is identical to above: https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/base/versions/3.6.2/topics/log Excel: log() defaults to base10, which is different! Either plug in the above i.e., log(val, EXP(1)), or use LN() ————— Python Notebook for GLR Poisson import os import arcpy import numpy as np import pandas as pd import statsmodels.api as sm import statsmodels.formula.api as smf # Current ArcGIS Pro project aprx = arcpy.mp.ArcGISProject("CURRENT") # List maps m = aprx.listMaps()[0] # Access table table = m.listTables("la_near_table_v2")[0] table_path = table.dataSource workspace = arcpy.Describe(ta # List of fields fields = [f.name for f in arcpy.ListFields(table_path)] # Data to pd rows = arcpy.da.SearchCursor(table_path, fields) df = pd.DataFrame(list(rows), columns=fields) # Create formula and run glm model_formula = "flow_int ~ C(ORIG_LAD21CD) + orig_pop_log + dest_gdp_log + near_dist_log - 1" model = smf.glm(formula=model_formula, data=df, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit() # Summary # print(model.summary()) # Predictions df["gm3"] = model.predict(exog=df) # Sets path to output (workspace + file name) #out_table = os.path.join(workspace, "la_near_table_v2_pred.csv") # Manual export to csv df.to_csv(r"C:/Users/mzdi2mt2/Documents/GEOG73001-processing/week-10/la_near_table_v2_pred.csv", index=False) 10.4 Resources Wilson, A.G. (1971). A family of spatial interaction models, and associated developments. Environment and Planning A, 3(1), pp.1-32. Haynes, K.E., & Fotheringham, A.S. (1985). Gravity and Spatial Interaction Models. Reprint. Edited by Grant Ian Thrall. WVU Research Repository, 2020. Fotheringham, A.S. and M.E. O’Kelly (1989) Spatial Interaction Models: Formulations and Applications. London: Kluwer Academic. Dennett, A. and Wilson, A. (2013). A multilevel spatial interaction modelling framework for estimating interregional migration in Europe. Environment and Planning A, 45(6), pp.1491-1507. Rowe, F., Lovelace, R. and Dennett, A. (2024). Spatial interaction modelling: a manifesto. In A research agenda for spatial analysis (pp. 177-196). Edward Elgar Publishing. Liao, M. and Oshan, T.M. (2025). Summary Closing remarks John Snow’s cholera map of Soho, London In 1854, Dr. John Snow … TALK ABOUT spatial analysis, key principles, methods change — often the questions don’t. Hope that students are equipped with the ability to do spatial analysis, but more importantly, really understand how the techniques work - the data, the assumptions, the mathematics… all of which will enable you to critically appraise your results and those of others, and ultimately to come a better understanding of spatial patterns, processes and relationships. Introduction to Spatial Analysis, we have covered… Acknowledgments The development of Introduction to Spatial Analysis has benefited from fruitful discussions with Jonny Huck and Matt Dennis, testing by James Tomlinson, and wider support from the Mapping, Computing and Geographical Information Science research group. The design of the practical materials has also been improved thanks to resources made available by the wider GIS community, including Sergio J. Rey, Dani Arribas-Bel and Levi J. Wolf (Geographic Data Science with Python), Luc Anselin (An Introduction to Spatial Data Science with GeoDa), Edzer Pebesma and Roger Bivand (Spatial Data Science with Applications in R), and Adam Dennett (Guide to Spatial Interaction Modelling). I hope that Introduction to Spatial Analysis has inspired you to explore the topic further, and if so, I’d recommend delving into the resources above, alongside the wider literature included in the reference list.
